title: Ricciarelli
subtitle: Sienese soft almond biscuits with orange zest
excerpt: Ricciarelli are the traditional almond biscuits of Siena: soft, chewy, fragrant with orange zest and almond extract, dusted thickly with icing sugar. They are naturally gluten-free, made only from ground almonds, icing sugar, egg whites, and citrus. The surface cracks as they bake, a sign of the right texture inside. They are closely related to French macarons and Spanish amaretti but are more rustic and forgiving. Allow them to cool completely before eating; they harden into a fudge-like chew.
sourceNotes: Ricciarelli are native to Siena, Tuscany, and their origins are traced to the medieval Arab influence on Sienese confectionery; almond sweets appear in Sienese records from the fourteenth century. A 1350 document mentions ricciarelli being served at the Sienese court. The name may derive from the Italian riccio (curly) referring to the cracked surface. They are now a presidio of the Slow Food movement and a IGP (Protected Geographical Indication) product.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dough can be made 24 hours ahead and refrigerated. The baked biscuits keep in an airtight container for up to 2 weeks and improve after 2 to 3 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; low temperature preserves the soft interior

content.text: The dough is very wet and sticky (more of a thick paste than a rollable dough. It is shaped using two spoons or a piping bag into oval mounds on a tray lined with parchment dusted with icing sugar. The surface cracks as they bake because the icing sugar crust sets while the interior is still expanding. This cracking is the intended result: a smooth ricciarello was baked at too high a temperature or for too long. Rest them for at least 2 hours after baking before eating) they need time to firm up.

Mix together 250 g ground almonds, 200 g icing sugar, and 0.5 tsp baking powder. Add the zest of 1 orange, 0.5 tsp almond extract, and 0.5 tsp vanilla extract. Add 2 egg whites and mix to a soft, sticky paste. Rest the dough at room temperature for 2 hours (it firms slightly as the almonds absorb moisture. Heat the oven to 160 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with parchment and dust generously with icing sugar. Using two spoons, shape the dough into small ovals, each about 4 cm long, and place on the dusted trays. Dust the tops heavily with icing sugar. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until lightly golden and just set with visible cracks. Cool completely on the tray) they are fragile when warm. Transfer to an airtight container and leave for at least 2 hours before serving.

items.solution: The surface was not dusted with enough icing sugar, or the oven temperature was too high and the whole biscuit set simultaneously. Use a generous coating of icing sugar and bake at 160 degrees C, not higher.
They were overbaked. Ricciarelli should be soft and fudge-like in the centre. Remove from the oven when the surface has just cracked and the top is very lightly golden — they firm on cooling.

items.problem: Dough is too wet to shape
items.solution: Rest the dough for longer — up to 4 hours. If it is still very wet, add a tablespoon of ground almonds and mix in. The resting time is when the almonds absorb the egg white and the paste becomes handleable.
